contest.title: 2024-2025 ICPC, NERC, Southern and Volga Russian Regional Contest (Unrated, Online Mirror, ICPC Rules, Preferably Teams)

sources.notes: Read the complete current English statement, all examples, and limits: n <= 200000, 0 <= a_i <= 10^9, 3 seconds, 512 MB, answer modulo 998244353. The required sequence of segment OR values is numerically nondecreasing and equality is allowed.
Read problem D in the official ICPC mirror statement booklet (PDF page 5 of 19), including the formal partition definition and examples.

Read the complete official D solution section (PDF pages 75-91). It gives the sparse dp[i][x] state, O(log 10^9) distinct OR values for fixed boundaries, right-endpoint breakpoint lists, delayed range additions by sweep line, two-pointer merging, and the stated O(n log n log log n) complexity.

Read the full 2038D section and code. It independently derives an offline ordering by segment OR and endpoint, queries cut-position intervals with a Fenwick tree, and gives O(n log V log n) time. One prose sentence says XOR, but every formula, transition, and code operation uses OR.

The submission page visibly showed Accepted (1546 ms, 64 KB), and the complete source was read. It implements the same partition recurrence with maps for completed last-OR states and waiting pairs, corroborating the base state and transition semantics; its live-state bound was not reconstructed tightly enough to promote it as a reviewed variant.

Independently proved the partition recurrence, the O(31) OR-breakpoint bound, the sweep event invariant, and the offline Fenwick ordering. In-memory exhaustive checks compared direct enumeration of all cuts with the accepted online-state recurrence for every array over {0,1,2,3} with 1 <= n <= 6 (5460 arrays); all answers agreed.

annotation.summary: Count partitions of a length-n nonnegative integer array into nonempty contiguous segments such that the numerical bitwise-OR values of consecutive segments form a nondecreasing sequence, modulo 998244353.
annotation.constraints: Single test case; 1 <= n <= 200000; 0 <= a_i <= 10^9; time limit 3 seconds; memory limit 512 MB; modulus 998244353.
annotation.core_idea: Let dp[i][x] be the number of valid partitions of the prefix ending at i whose last segment OR is exactly x, with dp[0][0]=1. If the next segment starts at i+1 and has OR y, its contribution is P_i(y)=sum_{x<=y}dp[i][x]. For any fixed boundary, extending a segment changes its OR at most 30 times because every strict change sets a previously zero bit, so both dp[i] and the candidate y values are sparse. For each start i+1, represent the endpoints by intervals [L,R) on which OR(i+1..q)=y is constant, and range-add P_i(y) to state (q,y) over that interval. A forward sweep schedules +P_i(y) at L and -P_i(y) at R; at each q, aggregated events form the sparse dp[q] list. Sort/merge the OR keys and use two pointers to obtain all needed prefix sums P_q(y), then schedule the next intervals.
annotation.complexity: The official sweep-line solution states O(n log n log log n) time. There are O(nB) sparse OR states/events and O(nB) memory with B <= 31 for values up to 10^9. The independent offline Fenwick variant is O(nB log(nB) + nB log n) time and O(nB) memory.
tricks: Compress all segment OR values for a fixed left or right boundary; a strict change must introduce a new set bit.
Query a numerical prefix x <= y, not the bit-submask relation x submask y.
Turn every constant-OR endpoint interval into two delayed sweep events.
Merge sparse OR lists with two pointers to evaluate all prefix sums together.
In the offline variant, sweep states by (OR value, endpoint) and use cut-position ranges in a Fenwick tree.
uncertainty_points: The official slides specify the high-level event-list and two-pointer implementation and state O(n log n log log n), but do not spell out the exact low-level container; the payload records their stated bound rather than inventing a sharper one.
The independent blog has one isolated XOR typo in prose. Its equations and C++ code use bitwise OR throughout, so it does not create a correctness ambiguity.
The accepted map implementation is useful corroboration but is not a separate reviewed solution variant because the consulted source does not provide a tight worst-case bound on all live map states.
No new taxonomy tag is proposed; the existing partition-DP, state-compression, sweep-line, bitmask, offline, and Fenwick tags cover both reviewed approaches.
proof_risks: The transition condition is the ordinary integer comparison previous_OR <= current_OR; replacing it by a subset-of-bits test rejects valid partitions.
Equality is valid. Using a strict prefix sum x < y loses partitions with equal neighboring OR values.
dp[0][0]=1 is a sentinel for the empty prefix; it must allow the first segment even when its OR is zero.
For a fixed boundary, duplicate OR values must be merged so each maximal endpoint or cut interval is represented exactly once.
Sweep intervals are half-open: add at L and subtract at R. Reversing event order at an endpoint creates off-by-one contributions.
When constructing breakpoint lists from a neighboring boundary, preserve the minimum endpoint for every distinct OR before sorting by OR value.
In the offline Fenwick variant, sort equal-OR objects by increasing endpoint so states dp[j][v] are inserted before a later segment query that may legally follow them.
Fenwick ranges are over previous prefix endpoints l-1, so a left-endpoint interval [L,R] maps to [L-1,R-1].
All event additions and subtractions must be normalized modulo 998244353.

solution_variants.name: sparse-or-dp-sweep-events
solution_variants.summary: Use dp[i][last_or], compress all possible OR values into O(31)-sized lists, convert each next-segment constant-OR endpoint interval into add/remove events, and sweep endpoints while merging sparse lists to compute the required numerical OR-prefix sums.
solution_variants.complexity: Officially stated O(n log n log log n) time; O(n log 10^9) sparse states/events and memory.

solution_variants.name: offline-or-interval-fenwick
solution_variants.summary: For every right endpoint, enumerate each distinct suffix OR y and the contiguous interval of segment left endpoints producing it. Sort these objects by (y,right endpoint). A Fenwick tree over previous prefix endpoints stores all dp states whose last OR is at most the current y; a range query over the corresponding cut interval yields dp[right][y], which is then inserted at that endpoint.
solution_variants.complexity: O(nB log(nB) + nB log n) time and O(nB) memory, where B <= 31.

tags.evidence: The answer is built by cutting a prefix and appending one final segment; dp[i][x] records the last segment OR, and every transition sums exactly the earlier states x <= y.
Although x ranges up to about 2^30, for any fixed segment boundary only O(31) distinct OR values occur, reducing each DP layer and each transition target list to sparse breakpoint states.

A transition contributes the same value to every endpoint in a constant-OR interval, so the official algorithm schedules an addition at the interval start and its negation at the interval end and sweeps positions forward.
The logarithmic state bound relies specifically on monotonic bitwise OR: every strict extension change turns on at least one previously absent bit and no bit ever turns off.
At each sweep position, sorted sparse dp OR keys and sorted candidate next OR keys are merged once to compute all prefix sums sum_{x<=y}dp[i][x].
The alternative solution globally orders all compressed segment states by target OR and endpoint so that the nondecreasing-OR condition becomes an insertion/query order.
After ordering by OR, each candidate state needs a sum over a contiguous range of previous cut positions and then a point insertion at its endpoint, exactly the Fenwick range-sum/point-add pattern.
